data.title: KKR & Co. 'D' Mandatory Convertible Preferred Gets Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) Called its two non-convertible preferred stocks in 2021. Its “C” mandatory convertible was then converted on 9/15/23 into 1.17 common shares. Assuming none of the preferred had been retired early, that resulted in just over 23MM new shares being issued, increasing the share count by just 2.5%. Here, I will review KKR’s only remaining preferred, the KKR & Co. Inc 6.25% Series D Mandatory Convertible Preferred Stock (NYSE:KKR.PR.D) and whether it provides better exposure to KKR than the common stock.

Based on the current price of the common stock and mandatory convertible preferred stock and my value calculations, I rate the KKR.PR.D as a Buy as a means of owning KKR come 2028.

KKR & Co. Inc. review

Obviously the first step of due diligence is determining if having any exposure to KKR & Co makes for a good investment. Seeking Alpha describes KKR as (edited):

KKR & Co. Inc. is a private equity and real estate investment firm specializing in direct and fund of fund investments. It specializes in acquisitions, leveraged buyouts, management buyouts, credit special situations, growth equity, mature, mezzanine, distressed, turnaround, lower middle market, and middle market investments.

For middle market private equity it seeks to invest in companies with enterprise values between $200 million and $1 billion ($1000 million). The firm prefers to invest in a range of debt and public equity investing and may co-invest. It seeks a board seat in its portfolio companies and a controlling ownership of a company or a strategic minority position.

The firm typically holds its investment for a period of five to seven years and more and exits through initial public offerings, secondary offerings, and sales to strategic buyers. KKR & Co. Inc. was founded in May 1, 1976 and is based in New York, New York with additional offices across North America, Europe, Australia, Middle East and Asia Pacific.

Here is how KKR & Co describes their mission.

This strategy has led to a 18% CAGR in AUM.

Fee growth was even stronger with a 25% CAGR. While both paint a rosy picture, it has not consistently flowed to the bottom line as shown in the EPS data.

Looking at the balance sheet shows the preferred stock has 9.8x in Total Common Equity backing it, which has to be one of the best ratios I have seen!

Other Seeking Alpha contributors give KKR a Buy rating, the most recent is linked here.

KKR & Co. Inc 6.25% Series D Mandatory Convertible Preferred Stock review

Key points include:

  • the 6.25% coupon is fixed and cumulative
  • the issue receives the favorable 15% rate treatment
  • the issue cannot be Called before it converts on 3/1/28
  • the conversion settlement rate will be 0.3312 shares per unit if the then market price is equal to or greater than $150.9662 and 0.4140 shares per unit if the market price is equal to or less than $120.7729. For market prices between those values the settlement rate will be $50 divided by the market value.
  • the preferred shares are convertible any time at the holder’s option into 0.3312 shares of common stock.

As reference, KKR has been trading between $100 to $170 over the past year.

Common or Convertible: which is best?

  • Yield: Currently the common yields just .5%, and with the preferred close to 5.5%; it has a 5% advantage over the common.
  • Premium: If converted now, this is about 20%. If KKR is selling at the $120 lower price limit, then today's premium drops to around 9%. At the IPO, I estimated the premium was 11% based on the higher conversion ratio.
  • Mandatory conversion: Occurs on 3/1/28.

Assuming KKR doesn't change the common stock dividend, I calculated that PFD holders will receive about $6.50 more in dividends before the conversion occurs. At the prices today, you pay $58 for the PFD that is worth only $49 in KKR common. Add in the dividend difference, buying the PFD today and waiting for the conversion works out to paying $58 today for a converted value of $55.50. If I did the math right, a KKR common price of only $156 at conversion is the breakeven price. Investors who believe KRR common will be higher than $156 on 3/1/28 should forgo the extra payments and own KKR directly. Again, if my understanding is correct, the PFD should not close below $50 on 3/1/28 as long as KKR trades above $120.77 at the time. If correct, adjusting for the extra payouts PFD holders will receive, owning the PFD seems to have less than $2/share of value risk until then assuming the price of KKR is above the lower reference price.

Author's note: Calculating the conversion premiums was done based on my understandings. Those with more knowledge are welcome to provide input on this critical part of the analysis.

Conclusion

Since it was issued in March, the preferred has paralleled the price movement in the common stock, with the common pulling ahead recently.

One way to look at the convertible PFD is as a long-term Call option that pays holders $3.125 yearly until the conversion date. With little risk on the downside unless KRR common collapses and with a breakeven price just $8 higher than today, I decided to rate the PFD as a Buy over owning the common stock.

Portfolio strategy

Convertible securities (bond or preferred stocks) are a way for companies to raise capital at a lower cost than a similar issue without that feature, especially if the conversion provision is mandatory as is the case here. Convertibles are even better than issuing perpetual preferred stocks that can be called or bonds that mature, as no cash flows out if/when those events happen. While conversion dilutes other investors’ ownership percent and can lead to lower earnings per share, the interest cost reduction might cause the eps drop not to happen.

For investors who want to add convertibles to their asset allocation but don’t want to spend the effort to build a diversified set, here are two funds with good 10-year CAGRs.

  • Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (CCD): 9.28%
  • Fidelity Convertible Securities Fund (FCVSX): 9.34%.

data.title: Did The Fed Really Deliver A Hawkish Hold? The FX Market Seems To Think So
data.description:
Overview

The dollar jumped yesterday on the back of rising rates as if despite the two dovish dissents by governors, yesterday's FOMC meeting was a hawkish hold. It seemed to us that Fed Chair Powell referred to downside risks more than upside risks. The dollar's pullback today has been limited, and the consolidation looks more like the breath that refreshes rather than the end of the short covering rally that began at the start of the month. President Trump announced a several new tariff schedules as the August 1 deadline looms. Note too that the arguments in the appeal case that challenge the legality of the reciprocal tariffs will be heard today. Despite stronger than expected economic data, the yen is the weakest in the G10. The BOJ stood pat as expected. The dollar is trading above the 200-day moving average for the first time since mid-February.

Outside of Japan and Taiwan, the large bourses in the Asia Pacific region fell, led by more than 1% drops in Hong Kong and China mainland. Disappointing PMI data appeared to weigh on activity. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is nursing small losses, while strong earnings by Microsoft and Meta is helping lift US index futures. Nasdaq futures are trading about 1.3% higher and the S&P 500 futures are up almost 1%. Benchmark 10-year yields are 1-3 bp lower in Europe and the 10-year US Treasury yield is off nearly two basis points to around 4.35%. Gold has snapped back. It lost 1.5% yesterday, the most in a month, and is around 1% higher now to resurface above $3300. September WTI is consolidating after it reached a new high for the month yesterday near $70.50. It is near session lows (~$69.40) in late European morning turnover.

USD: The Dollar Index rally was extended to almost 100.00 yesterday, its best level since the end of May. It settled above its upper Bollinger Band (~99.25) for only the second time this year (first was on May 12). It is consolidating after yesterday's surge and held above 99.50. The next technical objective may be near 100.60. Despite two governor dissents from the Fed's decision to standpat, the market took away a hawkish message. US rates rose. The dollar rallied. Stocks were sold. At Tuesday's settlement, the market had 17.5 bp of easing discounted and at yesterday's close a little less than 12. The June personal income and consumption data was embedded in yesterday's GDP report. The deflators may attract some attention, but the CPI/PPI steals the thunder, and Fed will see the July and August series before the next FOMC meeting. In fact, the Jackson Hole conference at the end of August (21-23) is on labor markets in transition is the next Fed highlight. The quarterly employment cost index is expected to have risen by 0.8% after a 0.9% rise in Q1. Weekly initial jobless claims have fallen for six consecutive weeks, but economists expect the streak to be snapped. Still, the national employment tomorrow has more heft to move the markets. Lastly, we note that the appellate arguments in the trade court case that found the tariffs imposed under "emergency" legislation are an executive branch overreach. Regardless of the eventual decision, it will likely be appealed again.

Euro With yesterday's losses, the euro fell through the June 23 low near $1.1455. The session low was set toward the end Fed Chair' Powell's press conference to almost $1.1400. It is holding today, but the euro recovery stalled near $11.460. The June low was set early in the month around $1.1340 and that may be the next technical target. Still, the euro settled below its lower Bollinger Band (~$1.1515) for the third time this year. The previous two times saw the euro begin to recover the following day. The bounce at the very start of the Fed's press conference met strong selling as it popped above $1.1500. After the eurozone reported a 0.1% growth in Q2, attention turns to inflation. Spain reported yesterday. The EU harmonized CPI measure fell by 0.4% but the base effect meant that the year-over-year pace rose to 2.7% from 2.3%. France reported today that the harmonized measure rose by 0.3% for a 0.9% year-over-year pace. Italian inflation has a strong seasonal pattern toward moderating in July and this year is no exception. The 1.0% decline month-over-month was the largest decline since the 0.9% drop last July. The year-over-year rate eased to 1.7% from 1.8%. German states have reported, and the national figure is due shortly. The harmonized measures may have risen by 0.4% for a 1.9% year-over-year pace (2.0% in June). The aggregate figure for the eurozone will be reported tomorrow. Today it reported that for the third consecutive month, the unemployment rate was at 6.2% in June, matching the record low under monetary union.

CNY: The dollar posted its biggest gain against the yuan since April yesterday, rising by about 0.45%. It rose and settled above CNH7.21 for the first time since June 2. It reached about CNH7.2145. It is consolidating between CNH7.1960 and CNH7.2115. The extent of further gains seems to depend on the greenback's broad movement. The next important chart area may be around CNH7.2240-65. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1494 (CNY7.1441 yesterday). Last week's absolute value of changes in the daily fix was 0.27% and with today's fix the absolute value of changes over the past four sessions is 0.30%. Early this year, the weekly changes were a quarter as much. While critics drew attention to it then, the widening has hardly been recognized. Nevertheless, it is likely to have been observed by Beijing that despite the greater flexibility of the dollar reference rate, the implied three-month volatility (a common benchmark) fell below 3.6% yesterday, the lowest since the end of July 2024. China's July PMI was a little softer than expected. The manufacturing PMI slipped to 49.3 from 49.7. It has not been above the 50 boom/bust level since March. It finished last year at 50.1. The non-manufacturing PMI stands at 50.1, down from 50.5. It was 52.2 in December 2024. The composite measure (output) eased to 50.2 from 50.7. It has risen in the previous two months.

JPY: The dollar posted a bullish outside up day against the yen. Initially, Tuesday's low (~JPY148.15) was taken out in local hours as the earthquake gave rise to ideas that insurers may need to repatriate funds. However, as US yields rose and the greenback extended its advance broadly. It took out Tuesday's high (~JPY148.80) and reached slightly above JPY149.50, a new high for the month. At JPY149.40, the dollar retraced this year's losses (from ~JPY158.85 on Jan 10). The dollar is near session highs around JPY149.80 late in the European morning. It has risen through the 200-day moving average (~JPY149.60) for the first time since mid-February to approach the psychologically important JPY150. Japan's June retail sales rose by 1.0%, well above expectations and recouping fully the 0.6% decline (revised from the initial -0.2%, in May). Industrial output jumped by 1.7%, while the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was for a 0.8% decline. by 0.8%, as expected. It was the third consecutive decline and the fourth in H1 25. As widely anticipated, the Bank of Japan left its target rate at 0.50%. It updated its GDP and core CPI forecasts. Growth was seen at 0.6% this year (up from 0.5%0), 0.7% the next year and 1.0% the following year (both unchanged). The core CPI projection was raised to 2.7% (from 2.2) and 1.8% (from 1.7% the next year) and 2.0% (from 1.9%) the following year. The swaps market 17-18 bp of tightening this year discounted, the least since last Tuesday and down a couple of basis points today.

GBP: Sterling posted a bearish outside down day. It traded on both sides of Tuesday's range and settled below its low (~$1.3310). It reached a low slightly below $1.3230, its lowest level since mid-May. The upside stalled near the five-day moving average, and a little above the neckline of the head and shoulders top pattern we have been monitoring. It projects to around $1.2940 and the halfway point of this year's range. It also settled below its lower Bollinger Band (~$1.3270). It bounced to about $1.3280 today, where sellers emerged to drive it back toward $1.3235. There seems little chart support ahead of the $1.3140 area. With little new data between now and August 7, there is practically nothing that will shake market expectations of a rate cut next week. The last time that the swap market was not discounting at least a 90% chance of a cut was two weeks ago.

CAD: The US dollar rose for the fifth consecutive session against the Canadian dollar, the longest advance since late February/early March. The greenback rose and settled above CAD1.38 for the first time since the end of May. It reached CAD1.3845 yesterday, leaving little on the charts to deter from a test on CAD1.3900. After a shallow pullback (to CAD1.3815), the US dollar is pushing against yesterday's high. The Bank of Canada left its target rate at 2.75%. The swaps market had about a 66% chance another cut will be delivered at the end of the year, and it has eased slightly to around 62% today. The central bank's new forecast is sobering. Under the current tariffs, the central bank projects a 1.5% annualized contraction in Q2, which is deeper than the market expects, but forecasts a rebound in Q3 of 1%. Growth this year is seen at 1.3% and 1.1% next year. In the January report, the central bank had anticipated 1.8% growth this year and next. It assumes the US tariff rate of 25% for only those goods that are not exempt under the USMCA. Tariffs on steel, aluminum, and non-US content imported vehicles are assumed to remain in place, as will Canada's retaliatory tariffs. It continued to hold out the possibility of another cut if a weakening economy exerts downward pressure on inflation.

AUD: The Australian dollar also fell for the fifth session yesterday. The streak began last Thursday when it was turned back after making a new high for the year (~$0.6625). It fell to almost $0.6425 yesterday, its lowest level since June 23. It has recovered to $0.6475 today but is hovering around $0.6450-5 in late European morning turnover. Nearby support may be seen around $0.6400, and the 200-day moving average is slightly below (~$0.6390). Australia reported a slew of data today, but none of it is enough to dissuade the market from its high conviction that the central bank will deliver its third quarter-point rate cut on August 12. Building approvals were a whopping 11.9% in June and well above expectations for a 1.8% gain. It was the second consecutive monthly gain after three straight drops. Retail sales jumped 1.2% in June, three times more than the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey. After adjusting for inflation, retail sales rose 0.3% in Q2 after a revised 0.1% gain in Q1 (initially flat). Private sector credit grew by 0.6%, which is about the average pace seen this year. Lastly, import and export price indices fell in Q2. Export prices fell 4.5% after rising by 2.1% in Q1. Import prices slipped by 0.8% after rising 3.3% in Q1.

MXN: The dollar traded on both sides of Tuesday's range against the peso yesterday but settled above its high. The dollar rose slightly above MXN18.87 in late turnover. It took out the July 15 high (~MXN18.8850) slightly, before pulling back to almost MXN18.7830. Still, it has turned better bid in Europe and recovered through MXN18.85. Above MXN18.8850, the next of note is around MXN18.93, the (50%) retracement of the dollar's last leg down from the June 23 high (~MXN19.3430). Mexico surprised with a 0.7% expansion in Q2. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was for 0.4% growth after 0.2% in Q1. However, the impact on the peso was overwhelmed by the US dollar's broad gains. As widely anticipated, Brazil's central bank left the Selic rate steady at 15.0%. The market expects the next move to be a cut. It was a volatile day for the exchange rate, but the dollar settled slightly weaker on the day near BRL5.57. News that America's 50% tariff was going to delayed by a week and that exemptions, including orange juice, oil products, natural gas, aircraft, and machinery, helped the real recover from early losses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Medtronic: Growth Challenges Persist
data.description:
Company Description

Based in Ireland, Medtronic plc (NYSE:MDT) is a medical device manufacturer with operations throughout the world. At this time, the company has four reportable segments; Cardiovascular, Medical Surgical, Neurosciences, and Diabetes. However, a few months ago, management announced they would be spinning off the Diabetes segment into its own separate company. Medtronic sports a current market cap of nearly $120B and employs close to 100,000 people worldwide. Over the past decade, the company has struggled with its identity some, and in turn the stock has suffered, returning just 55%, or about 4.5% annually, well-below the broad market. Hopefully the company can refocus following the Diabetes spin-off and return to growth.

Quality Financial Metrics

As usual, I'm going to examine some important quantitative financial metrics for MDT and assess how the company has performed. I'll be looking for a growing revenue stream, a reliable gross profit margin, and a healthy return on invested capital.

The revenue per share metric for MDT has grown minimally since FY2016, about 2.7% per year, on average. Aside from the double-digit growth in FY16, the RPS growth has consistently been in the low-to-mid-single-digit range. FY25 was the best mark (aside from 2016), and hopefully it's a sign of things to come for the company. Lastly, the company does have a share repurchase program and typically repurchases anywhere from 1% to 2% of its shares per year, in fact in FY25 more than 2.2% of shares were repurchased.

The gross profit margin for Medtronic has been declining slowly, going from a peak of 70.3% in FY19, to a low last fiscal year where the GPM came in at 65.4%. The company stated in its most recent earnings release changes in foreign exchange rates have contributed slightly to the GPM contraction; but the main culprit has been the product mix from Diabetes and Cardiac Solutions have a been a significant factor. In all likelihood, this contributed to management deciding to break off the Diabetes segment, time will tell if they are making the right move.

The return on invested capital metric has oscillated quite a bit over the past ten years, with a peak in FY22 of 8.3%, and a low in FY18 of just 4.6%. Similar to the GPM, the aforementioned spin off is likely to help this metric rise as the company focuses on more lucrative and higher-margin growth segments.

Dividend Data

In May of this year, Medtronic announced its 48th consecutive dividend increase, raising its dividend a meager 1.4%. The company now pays a quarterly dividend of $0.71 per share, or $2.84 annually, and yields a little more than 3%. Although the 10-year dividend growth rate sits at a respectable 7%, the growth has clearly slowed as indicated by the blue line, with the last two increases coming in at just 1.5% and 1.4%. And to be fair, it's probably a smart decision at the payout ratio (orange line) has been on the rise and nearly surpassed 100% in FY24. I'd expect the increases to be minimal for the foreseeable future until the company can return to at least somewhat of a growth phase.

Earnings Update

In late May, MDT announced their Q4 earnings. Normalized EPS was $1.62 per share, on revenue of $8.9B, which topped analyst expectations by $100M, and was a 4% YoY increase. Additionally, GAAP EPS was $0.82 which came up well short of estimates.

The Cardiovascular segment earned revenue of $3.33B an increase of 6.6% overall, and 7.8% organically. The Cardiac Rhythm & Heart Failure division grew thanks to continued adoption of the PulseSelect and Affera mapping and ablation system, while Cardiac Rhythm Management also continued its success with near double-digit growth in Defibrillation Solutions and Cardiac Pacing Therapies.

The Neuroscience segment has sales of $2.6B resulting in an overall increase of 2.9%, and 3.7% organically. Within the Neuroscience segment, Cranial & Spinal Technologies improved as a result of strong U.S. growth. Conversely, Neurovascular faced procurement issues in China, as well as a recall of the Pipeline Vantage flow diverter.

Medical Surgical struggled with revenue of $2.2B, a 0.6% overall increase, and 2% organic increase. The Surgical & Endoscopy piece of the Medical Surgical segment saw modest growth, driven by Advanced Energy with the LigaSure vessel sealing technology still showing strong demand, while Advanced Stapling was a headwind for this area due to ongoing bariatric market and competitive robotic pressures.

And finally, Diabetes saw the most YoY improvement with sales rising 10.4% as reported, and 12% organically, to $728M for the quarter. Revenue in the United States grew nicely, on the increased adoption of the MiniMed 780G automated insulin delivery system. More impressively, International grew by double-digits as growth in pumps and overall product upgrades saw advancement.

The market reacted poorly to the earnings release with shares dropping more than 4%. However, since the earnings release until now, MDT's stock has risen by more than 5%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

The next earnings release is Tuesday, August 19th. According to Seeking Alpha, Medtronic has exceed EPS estimates in 15 out of the last 16 quarters, while revenue numbers haven't been quite as impressive, topping the consensus in 11 out of the last 16 quarters.

Valuation

The last consideration is whether MDT is trading for an attractive valuation, and as usual, we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more insight as to how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates a slight undervaluation right now, with the stock trading for about $91, and a fair price closer to $96. The free cash flow for MDT has gone and up down, although fairly minimally, over the past few years. And similarly, the stock has followed suit with some good and bad years along the way. Medtronic needs to start growing its FCF, even if it's slowly, to give investors confidence the company is at least heading in the right direction.

The long-term estimated rate of return for MDT is a respectable 9.60%, with the pieces of that projection being a forward dividend yield of 3.1%, a return to fair value factor of 0.98%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 5.52%

Final Thoughts

In summary, MDT has struggled mightily during the past decade, with returns well-below the average market return. The revenue per share metric has been fairly stagnant, with minimal growth, while the gross profit margin and ROIC have contracted. The dividend history is the most impressive piece of the company, with almost 50 years of dividend growth; however, that growth has slowed to just the bare minimum to keep the company's dividend streak alive. The recent earnings report was mixed, with some segments performing better than others. Although the valuation is somewhat attractive right now, I'd prefer to see a decline in the stock, to give myself a better margin of safety before possibly initiating a position...for now I'll stay on the sidelines and wait for a better entry point.

data.title: Icahn Enterprises: Next Leg Down Should Be Close For This 21% Yielder (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
After being bearish on Icahn Enterprises LP (NASDAQ:IEP) for almost all of 2024, we upgraded it from a "strong sell" to a "hold" in December. We felt the time was right, what with the stock having declined by over 35% during the year. We elaborated on the two key reasons for this upgrade in that piece, neither of which had to do with the company fundamentals.

The fundamentals for IEP are extremely weak. The company is leveraged and its primary source of cash (the refiner known as CVI) is in deep distress. Its bonds are also trading at fairly wide spreads, adjusted for their credit rating. In other words, the market perceives a distress even worse than the rating agencies. The bleed on the cash flow will need to stop soon, and the distribution is going bye-bye in 2025. Of course, it could pay a token distribution, but another 90% cut, at the minimum, looks guaranteed.

Source: Icahn Enterprises: A Good Point For Shorts To Declare Victory

After two cuts, roughly a year apart, the dividends were 25% of what they were in May 2023.

Based on the math, even the $2 annual payout was unsustainable. Well, the company is still doling out over 21% in annualized yield ($9.34 current price), resulting in a chunky 16.21% total return over the last seven months. The price increase played a cameo in this feel good story.

What are the wings beneath this lofty payout? Has the foundation become even weaker with the company's refusal to cut? Lets review the numbers and find out.

Losses, Losses As Far As The Eye Can See

IEP reported another bad quarter with losses nearly reaching $425 million. That is a stunning number in context of what is happening elsewhere in the equity markets. Sure, the markets had a tough April, but these numbers are still end of March. The rest of the figures did not soothe your senses either.

Q1 2025 net loss attributable to IEP was $422 million, compared to a loss of $38 million in Q1 2024

Q1 2025 Adjusted EBITDA loss attributable to IEP was $287 million, compared to Adjusted EBITDA attributable to IEP of $134 million in Q1 2024

Indicative Net Asset Value was approximately $3.0 billion as of March 31, 2025, a decrease of $336 million compared to December 31, 2024.

Source: IEP Press Release

We have often harped that the bulls need to take the NAV number straight from the horse's mouth to figure out just exactly how much they were overpaying for this. The indicative NAV, while generous, is enough to induce nausea in most value investor stomachs. How does the overall NAV translate into NAV per unit though? The income statement shows 523 million units outstanding, a robust 100 odd million increase over the previous year. That though is the weighted average number. But the actual amount is the one below.

As of May 7, 2025, there were 546,086,101 of Icahn Enterprises’ depositary units outstanding.

Source: IEP 10-Q

So you have the company's own stated NAV now showing at $5.50 per unit. To put further context here, debt to asset value is also beginning to look rather ominous.

As of March 31, 2025, our Holding Company had cash and cash equivalents of approximately $1.3 billion and total debt of approximately $4.7 billion. As of March 31, 2025, our Holding Company had investments in the Investment Funds with a total fair market value of approximately $2.5 billion.

Source: IEP Press Release

Outlook & Verdict

IEP deterioration continues at a fast pace. The unit/share count is now going absolutely parabolic.

The company's NAV drop now makes it impossible to even fund half of this reduced distribution in cash. Think about it this way. Which company can generate 40% returns on equity consistently? Certainly not one that has a chart looking like this.

The key question is whether there is anything left here to salvage. It is obvious that the income chasers are fine playing the musical chairs game to see who actually lands up with without a seat. The next distribution cut, or perhaps an elimination, is likely to happen fairly soon. At that point, you would expect this to move back to below NAV and stay there. On our previous take, we had put a lot of emphasis on the extremely oversold nature and bearish positioning. That led us to conclude that while the longer-term downside was intact, this was not going anywhere any time soon. One key indicator we look at where is the stock in relation to its 200-day moving average. We use this for both entry and exit points. Late last year, IEP was almost 40% below its 200-day moving average.

While the price has not increased much, the 200-day moving average has caught up. The price now sits just 5% below it. The NAV per unit has also dropped appreciably in this timeframe as the stock has moved higher. In other words, the premium has increased significantly. We think the next down leg is now very close. Alongside the very overbought general market conditions, IEP is a very risky stock today. We are downgrading this to a "Strong Sell".

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: L'Oreal: Encouraging Green Shoots, But Not Enough
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCF), for which I recommended a hold rating as I was worried about the weak demand in China, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. Post reviewing 2Q25 results, although there are some green shoots emerging in key markets, the recovery strength remains weak, and several product-level headwinds have intensified. As such, I maintain a hold rating.

2Q25 Earnings Review

LRLCF 2Q25 results were mixed, with both positives and negatives. On the positive side, like-for-like sales growth was 2.4%, which, if adjusted for the phasing impact from its global IT systems upgrade, would mean organic growth would've accelerated from 2.6% in 1Q25 to 3.7% in 2Q25. However, performance was not even across divisions. Professional Products did well, growing 11.5% y/y, while Consumer Products also held up well with 3.3% y/y growth. However, the Luxe division declined 1.9% y/y, and dermatological beauty also decelerated to 3.5% y/y growth. Profitability, however, was a bright spot. 1H25 EBIT margin expanded 30 bps to a record 21.1%. Altogether, adj. net profit was up 1%.

Green Shoots Emerge in Key Markets

The biggest source of optimism in this set of results is the clear recovery in two key geographies that were previously major drags: North America and Mainland China. In my last write-up, I pointed to North America's broad-based weakness as a top concern. That dynamic has flipped for the better. Like-for-like growth in the region accelerated to 8.3% y/y in 2Q25, with management pointing to improving consumer demand and a recovery across channels.

Even more encouraging is that Mainland China returned to positive growth (remember this was one of the main reasons I downgraded to hold). After adjusting for IT phasing, management commented that China sales flipped from a slight decline in 1Q25 to around 3% y/y in 2Q25. There are clear signs of demand recovery, and a good example is LRLCF's strong performance during the 6.18 festival, where several brands posted double-digit growth. Coupled with strength in emerging markets like SAPMENA-SSA (10.4% y/y) and Latin America (10.3% y/y), it does suggest LRLCF has moved past the trough and is heading towards stabilization. This rebound is especially important because it comes at a time when Europe was relatively weak.

There are still lingering weaknesses

Despite the improvements seen in those key markets, there are still several headwinds I previously noted that have not eased. In fact, some of them have worsened. Most notably, the weakness faced in Luxe has turned for the worse. As mentioned, Luxe sales fell 1.9% y/y, and this is exceptionally worrisome because this was a core growth driver for the business. The main cause here is the weakness in Asia's travel retail channel, which I don't expect to see a strong recovery anytime soon. The underlying operating metrics are just not showing any signs of recovery. Specifically, the LRLCF own travel retail business is declining at a pace of low teens y/y, and the overall travel retail channel remains in negative growth of high teens. Conditions in Hainan are not looking any better, with sell-out down ~25%, suggesting that this drag will persist through 2H25 and continue to weigh on both Luxe and North Asia.

Aside from Luxe, even dermatological beauty, which has been the division that has consistently delivered double-digit growth in recent years, saw a sharp deceleration. Growth slowed to just 3.5% y/y in 2Q25, and while management attributed that decline to a tough comp from last year, it doesn't change the fact that demand is soft in North Asia-which management noted is making it difficult to regain momentum. Elsewhere, Europe also continued to underperform, with 2Q25 growth at just 2.4% y/y, and France was cited as particularly weak.

If the fundamental demand problem is not enough, there is now an external headwind that makes the entire equity story even worse than it already is. The US tariffs-a recent agreement will impose a 15% tariff on US imports of EU beauty products-are highly material for LRLCF Luxe and fragrance segments. This is because while LRLCF generally manufactures most of its products in the regions where they are sold, its high-value luxury and fragrance products are not. ~30% of the products LRLCF sells in the US are imported from Europe, and these imports are mostly within Luxury and fragrances. Management specifically highlights this vulnerability, stating this: "The one exception are luxury fragrances, which are made in Europe." While management believes the impact will be contained to <40 bps on EBIT margin, this adds another layer of uncertainty and introduces an incremental headwind that could weigh on 2H25 profitability.

Valuation

LRLCF is certainly not dwelling in the trough of this demand cycle anymore, as seen from the recovery in China and the US (two key regions). Hence, I think it makes sense that the market pushes up the stock's valuation from the low of ~25x in late 2024 to 28x today. But I think optimism is likely capped at this level because the fact of the matter is that LRLCF is still facing multiple weaknesses that are weighing down on earnings growth. Remember that 1H25 only grew earnings by 1%. Given the pressure from the tariffs and demand softness in key product lines, I don't think the near-term earnings outlook is going to be great, and for that, I don't think the market will re-rate this stock any higher until there is more evidence of earnings growth acceleration.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for LRLCF is hold. While the turnaround in China and the US is encouraging, issues in Luxe, new tariff risks, and poor earnings growth limit the upside, in my view. Until I see a clear recovery in high-margin categories like luxury and dermatological beauty and more conviction that earnings can grow meaningfully, I don't think the stock deserves to re-rate meaningfully from current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A 16% That's Set To Grow: Dynex
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Size can have a massive impact on many different aspects of our daily lives.

If you're sailing towards a hurricane, would you rather be in a cruise ship or a speedboat?

If you want to win the race off the line at the green light, you likely would rather be on a motorcycle than in a transport truck.

Size can benefit you or be a detriment to you, depending upon the circumstances you find yourself in. As a small investor, you may be able to squeak in and out of different trading opportunities more readily than someone with a portfolio of substantial size that would take longer to enter or exit a position.

When it comes to companies in the market, having exposure to both small, nimble, agile options and the behemoths that have decades of lifespan and experience behind them can benefit your portfolio. Very few sectors have been as misunderstood and misaligned as the mortgage real estate investment trust sector. mREITs have been with us for a long time and yet are still readily misunderstood in how they operate. This can be partially attributed to the fact that instead of paying variable dividends, they usually pay regular ones. This means that they have to cut or raise, and it causes ripples through their entire shareholder base. It would be better, in my opinion, if they were to pay variable dividends, paying out what they can afford to pay out, and adjust them quarterly as earnings change, thereby avoiding the confusion that currently exists.

Today, I want to look at a smaller option. The management team behind it is pivotal to perform strongly versus its peers.

Let's dive in!

Pedal to the Metal

Dynex Capital (NYSE:DX), yielding 16%, is an mREIT. DX is a small peer to larger options like AGNC (AGNC) and Annaly Capital (NLY). As a smaller peer, its portfolio is more dynamic and adaptable. This means management's choices can have an outsized impact on its overall performance. Since our last coverage, DX has performed strongly, so let's check in on it again.

DX reported a 4.9% decline in book value, which is the ballpark we expect to see most peers. This decline was primarily driven by the increase in the spread between MBS and hedges. With book value ending the quarter at $11.95, DX was trading above book value for more of the quarter and took advantage of that by raising $282 million through issuing common shares.

DX grew its portfolio by over $3 billion in Q2, which is very significant relative to its size. That is nearly a 28% increase: Source

The benefit of being smaller is that it is possible to expand the portfolio at a higher rate. DX management has clearly decided that it is an optimal time to be buying MBS. Their new purchases were focused on the 5.5% coupon, which now represents a third of their holdings.

In addition to issuing new equity, DX opted to be more aggressive with leverage. Going from 7.4x to 8.3x over the quarter:

On the cash flow side, DX saw a significant increase in interest income and net interest income. A trend that has been occurring for several quarters.

DX also continues to recognize gains from their hedges. The past few years, DX focused on shorting US Treasuries as a hedge, and they have chosen to defer those gains for taxes. As a result, they are recognizing approximately $25 million/quarter in gains. Their cumulative gains are currently $719 million.

These gains are not included in DX's Earnings Available for Distribution metric, however, they are considered REIT taxable income and represent an amount that has to be distributed to meet their distribution obligations.

As a result, EAD has been below DX's dividend at $0.22; however, we do not consider that a good indication of dividend safety since DX is required to distribute significantly more.

In the earnings call, management noted that their current dividend is sustainable even if the Fed does nothing, and that any action from the Fed to cut would increase returns further. COO/CFO Colligan said:

"With the steepening of the yield curve, the Agency RMBS market is currently offering positive carry, which doesn't require any action from the Fed or other market moves to deliver the levered yield to support our dividend.

Mortgage spreads remain wide and negative swap spreads adds to the long-term returns of the portfolio. And the reduction in financing costs later this year or in 2026 will be an additional boost to an already strong return."

We've previously discussed the spread between MBS and hedges as the way agency mREITs make money. The higher this spread, the lower the book value is, but also the higher the forward returns will be.

Here is a look at spreads since 1990:

Note that historically, the spread has widened during periods of distress like COVID, the GFC, and the Dot-Com bust. When it has widened to 175+ bps, it has historically been for a brief amount of time. As a result, agency mREITs haven't always been able to buy when spreads are this high because it was accompanied by mayhem throughout the financial system. Today, spreads are high and the financial system is relatively stable. mREITs can easily find counterparties to buy hedges from, and trade is happening at high volumes – as opposed to a period of panic where many institutions are going into lockdown mode.

Quite simply, this is hands down the best environment for buying agency MBS that has ever existed. The forward returns on MBS are higher today than they have ever been before, and they are remaining high on a sustained basis.

DX is putting the pedal to the metal on their growth, and with a generational opportunity in front of them, that is exactly what they should be doing.

Conclusion

When it comes to investing in MBS, most individual investors are not able to afford the diversification that one may want in a sector filled with highly expensive individual holdings. You most definitely can use different options to invest in MBS. We previously highlighted an option to invest in agency MBS with no leverage whatsoever via an ETF. You can use mREITs to invest in MBS as well, with various approaches and sizes. DX is an opportunity to get into the sector with a management team that believes in being agile and nimble. They navigate this smaller-than-peers company very effectively to reward their shareholders. They do use leverage, as almost any company that invests in MBS does, which provides a level of additional risk.

The question is, can they outperform with this higher risk than the asset base itself?

The answer is solidly yes.

When it comes to retirement, your portfolio should be a collection of wonderful investment opportunities. As an income investor, all of them should pay you solid dividends. My goal when building an income portfolio is to see strong income pouring in that floods my expenses and provides me with excess that I can enjoy and reinvest. The capital of my portfolio should steadily grow over time because of reinvestment and the growth of the companies I hold. We've seen all that and more within High Dividend Opportunities' Model Portfolio.

That's the beauty of my Income method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: 2 Elite Mispriced Dividend Stocks I'm Buying Now
data.description:
Introduction

What makes a good stock picker?

It's one of the most important questions to ask, because predicting economic scenarios (i.e., our Big Picture research) is only part of the job. Identifying the right investments is equally vital, as we won't make progress without turning great ideas into profits and consistent cash flows in the form of dividends.

The fascinating thing is that stock picking is super individual. As I have written in prior articles, there are literally tens of thousands of public companies we can pick from.

This also explains why the most popular investors all have super different portfolios. Bill Ackman owns asset managers, emerging industrials, and diversified fast-casual restaurants in his biggest holdings.

Meanwhile, Bill Gates has a large portfolio of supply-chain companies, including waste management services, railroads, and farm/construction machinery.

Chris Hohn from the TCI Fund Management is massively overweight in aerospace, technology, and financial services.

The one thing where all of these people agree is the principles that Benjamin Graham discussed, making him the Father of Value Investing. Regardless of whether we buy fast-growing tech stocks or slow-growing supply chain giants, the goal is always to buy a stock for less than we expect it to be worth.

Believe it or not, even people buying Palantir (PLTR) shares at more than 700x LTM earnings believe they can sell them for much more in the future. And to be honest, they could be right. Who knows?

The bigger the gap between the likely intrinsic value of the company and its market price, the bigger the margin of safety, and the better the risk/reward for investors seeking to optimize their portfolios by the addition of new stocks.

Warren Buffett achieves this by picking companies with competitive moats, meaning business models that are hard to disrupt and very likely to generate strong long-term value. By doing so, he focuses on "businesses" first before he assesses the "stocks."

I agree with that, as I tend to have a "never sell" mindset. I would never invest in a company if I didn't want to be willing to make it my only position if I absolutely had to. I call it the "ownership" mindset, where we assess investments as if we were a majority shareholder or its CEO/Chairman.

Then there's Peter Lynch, who makes the Buffett/Graham approach a bit more personal by advocating that we invest in what we know, meaning we can get an edge by finding opportunities that improve our daily lives. By doing so, he often uses the price-to-earnings growth ratio, which incorporates earnings growth into the traditional P/E ratio.

Here are three valuable lessons from Lynch:

1. Understand the Business Around Us: Investment success can start from simple things that we know and understand. Using a company's products or services can provide deeper insight into its growth potential.2. Don't Rely Too Much on Numbers: While financial analysis is important, a hands-on understanding of the business and product is also vital. Everyday observations can be an invaluable source of information.3. Trust Your Consumer Instincts: As consumers, we often have sharp instincts about quality products or services. Use these instincts as part of your investment strategy.

- Via AEI.

I like to combine all of these lessons when picking stocks. This blended approach means I'm not just crunching numbers or chasing growth for its own sake.

Instead, I concentrate on businesses I understand deeply. Businesses where I can see the competitive moat and the benefits of the product or service in our daily lives and major supply chains. This even includes companies I will never personally interact with, such as oil companies providing energy, data center engineers supporting the data center buildout, railroads fueling the global economy through their extensive networks, and defense contractors that are the backbone of NATO's defense capabilities.

When buying these businesses at a great price and secular growth tailwinds, I believe it is very hard to bet on the wrong horse.

In this article, I'll discuss two of my holdings. These are two of my favorite dividend (growth) stocks with fantastic business models and valuations that I expect will lead to outperformance for many years to come.

So, let's start with the most controversial one.

#1: L3Harris Technologies (LHX) - A Rally With A Lot More Room To Run

This company isn't controversial. What's controversial is that it is currently trading at an all-time high, which could make it seem very unlikely we're dealing with a mispriced opportunity.

When L3Harris was struggling, I pounded the table on this opportunity, as I was convinced the market was throwing the baby out with the bathwater, something that tends to happen occasionally.

  • Defense contractors, in general, were dealing with post-pandemic supply chain issues and pricing headwinds.
  • Budget uncertainty caused investors to avoid the industry.
  • New entrants like Andurel caused investors to fear that established players were about to rapidly lose market share.

While the risk of new entrants is always present, investor fears have been overblown, as most contractors have made new all-time highs in recent weeks, with L3Harris being one of them.

This company isn't being disrupted. L3Harris is the disruptor.

The company, which was established in 2019 through the merger of L3 Technologies and the Harris Corporation, specializes in all defense domains, as it focuses on advanced technologies that include resilient communications, space-based architectures, missile systems, and autonomous platforms.

Essentially, L3Harris positions itself as a "trusted disruptor," as it emphasizes agility, innovation, and alignment with the national security priorities of the U.S. and its allies. It does this by mainly operating as a supplier/partner to other defense companies, which allows it to win more deals without having to do the bidding itself.

Innovation comes from aggressive R&D and investments in dozens of startups, which allows L3Harris to benefit from other disruptors through their growth and the fact that it implements their technologies into its own products.

Moreover, by doing so, it doesn't have to spend a lot of money on large M&A deals, which leaves more free cash flow for shareholder distributions.

Even more important, because of its agility, the company is in perfect alignment with changing defense requirements, including areas like the Golden Dome initiative, which has become a critical homeland security program aimed at countering hypersonic threats.

It is basically a huge version of Israel's Iron Dome, which I covered in a special article this year. As some of you may remember, L3Harris was brought up in that article as well as a winner of what could be a >$1 trillion program.

In its latest Q2 '25 earnings call, the company noted it is preparing to deploy a constellation of 40–45 Hypersonic and Ballistic Tracking Space Sensors ("HBTSS") satellites, supported by investments in manufacturing facilities in Florida and Indiana.

Additionally, the company supports nearly all U.S. interceptor programs through its propulsion and control systems (see below), which was the benefit of buying Aerojet Rocketdyne, one of the smartest M&A deals I have seen.

Moreover, it benefits from strong international demand. While I believe most NATO members will fail to bring total defense spending to 5% of their GDP (or anything close to that, really), there is a surge in demand, which allows the company to use its overseas ties to win more orders.

That's why Q2 '25 was such a stellar quarter, as the company reported $8.3 billion in new orders, pushing the book-to-bill ratio to 1.5x, indicating $1.50 in new orders for every dollar of finished work. This is highly favorable for elevated future growth.

It also saw 6% organic revenue growth, the highest number in six quarters, and pushed its operating margin to 15.9%, the seventh-consecutive quarter of margin expansion, as the handy overview below shows.

These margins were boosted by LHX NeXt cost savings, higher volumes, more favorable contract conditions, and other minor tailwinds that all allowed L3Harris to prove it can finally grow both revenues and margins in a sustainable way.

The LHX NeXt program is a big deal, as it targets $1 billion in cost savings over three years. So far, the company is 40% ahead of schedule and focuses on advanced enterprise systems and AI-embedded tools to streamline.

Thanks to all of these tailwinds, the company raised its 2025 revenue guidance by 200 million, expecting at least 5% organic growth. Adjusted EPS guidance increased by $0.10 despite a $0.30 tax reform headwind. It also sees a $200 million tailwind in free cash flow guidance.

Even better, its 2026 guidance also saw a boost, as it raised free cash flow guidance to $3 billion, which is a 13% increase. In the 2023-2026 period, per-share free cash flow is expected to grow by 15%.

I don't think I need to add that this is a big deal.

With regard to its dividend, growth has been sluggish, as the most recent hike was just 3.4%, announced on February 28, 2025.

However, this is still due to the company working on reducing post-M&A debt. Now, it has reached its debt target and achieved a path to higher growth.

That's why its 1.8% yield is much more attractive than one might think, as the 40% payout ratio (within its target range) is now meeting higher growth.

Using FactSet data, analysts expect a strong rebound in EPS growth, with 17% and 14% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

Given the pace of order growth and margins, I expect these growth rates to last, justifying its current 23.1x earnings multiple.

It's also why I expect this misunderstood defense contractor to return 14-15% per year in the years ahead, potentially beating the market by a substantial margin.

L3Harris is my 10th-largest investment.

#2: Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNQ) - Because It's Great And I'm So Very Bullish On Energy

In recent weeks, I have spent a lot of time discussing energy.

My most recent article was titled "Drill, Baby, Drill: 9 Stocks With Enough Forepower To Outperform For Decades."

The article was about the attractive valuation of energy stocks (see below) and the end of the shale revolution, as America has run out of most Tier 1 drilling reserves, meaning that producers need to be more careful, as tapping lower-tier reserves means pressure on margins through higher production costs.

However, that's not bearish. In fact, it's bullish, as lower output from the one country that provided almost every drop of oil supply growth since 2009 means a much more resilient supply/demand balance.

This is fantastic news for companies with deep reserves, which benefit from potential pricing tailwinds and the ability to boost output.

Some of these companies are located in Canada's Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin, which is home to some of the largest oil and gas reserves on the planet.

Additionally, Canada's oil sands are different from American shale, as production often occurs in mines, meaning large trucks bring oil sands to facilities where chemicals separate the oil.

While these operations are super "dirty" (look them up on Google or other platforms) and come with high upfront costs to establish infrastructure, there are no decline rates and super low production costs.

Canadian National is Canada's largest oil producer and its second-largest natural gas producer. It has a reserve life index of more than 30 years, a number that has been steady for many years due to new discoveries.

The company, which expects to produce more than 1.1 million barrels of oil and other liquids per day this year, has a corporate decline rate of just 11%, as its oil sand operations account for more than a third of its output.

Moreover, it is super efficient, as its Thermal In Situ (below-surface oil sand operations) drilling is breakeven in the mid-$40 WTI range. Its oil sand mines are breakeven below $40 WTI, substantially better than its peers.

This bodes very well for shareholder returns.

The giant returned C$1.7 billion in capital in the first quarter, including C$1.2 billion in dividends and C$500 million in buybacks, which improved the per-share value of the company.

It also increased its dividend by 4%, which marks the 25th consecutive dividend hike with a long-term CAGR of 21%. Its five-year CAGR is 22.6%, as the company has made tremendous progress when it comes to boosting output and benefiting from better prices since the 2020 pandemic.

For what it's worth, the company is now a Dividend Aristocrat.

Currently, the company yields 5.5%.

It also has a fantastic balance sheet, as C$1.4 billion in free cash flow in 1Q25 allowed it to reduce the net debt ratio to just 1.0x EBITDA. It has north of C$5.0 billion in liquidity.

Also, and this may be hard to believe without evidence, over the past ten years, CNQ shares have beaten the S&P 500 (SP500) by a substantial margin, something I expect to continue going forward, as I am very bullish on oil and not very bullish on the top-heavy and rather expensive S&P 500.

Valuation-wise, the company trades at just 6.5x operating cash flow, a number substantially below its two-decade average of 8.2x, which I consider to be the bare minimum for a high-quality producer like Canadian Natural.

Including expectations of average double-digit per-share operating cash flow growth through at least 2027 (FactSet), we get a fair stock price of roughly C$70 in Canada, more than 60% above the current price.

The same applies to New York-listed shares, with the addition of CAD/USD currency risks.

Takeaway

Great stock picking isn't just about correctly predicting the Big Picture.

As important as that may be, it's about finding the right horses to bet on and building a strong portfolio.

In this article, I highlighted two of my favorites.

  • L3Harris, a misunderstood defense contractor that's quietly dominating next-gen warfare tech with record orders, expanding margins, and a business model primed for disruption.
  • Canadian Natural Resources, a low-cost energy giant with massive reserves, impressive dividend growth, and a balance sheet built for any cycle.

To me, these are businesses I would happily hold forever, as they come with moats, value, and tailwinds all in one.

I'm not trying to trade headlines or chase hype. I'm simply focused on building a portfolio of durable, cash-generating assets I can trust to deliver in good times and bad.

I think it's fair to say these two fit that bill perfectly.

And while my capital is tight after buying a lot more LandBridge (LB) this month, I am looking to add new capital to LHX and CNQ in the weeks ahead.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to L3Harris is defense budget uncertainty, severe supply chain challenges, and the inability to keep up with the latest technological developments.

Canadian Natural is prone to wildfires and operational risks, yet the biggest potential headwind is a prolonged downtrend in oil prices. While it has industry-beating breakeven costs, it could keep pressure on its stock price and dividend growth.

data.title: Alamos Gold: Record Financials In Q2 But Increased Cost Guidance For 2025
data.description:
Overview

Alamos Gold (NYSE:AGI) is a high-quality mid-cap gold mining company with most of its production in Canada and a minor amount of production in Mexico in the Mulatos District.

I have covered the company frequently over the last couple of years here on Seeking Alpha and my prior articles can be found here. Alamos Gold released its Q2 2025 result yesterday after the close, which this article will primarily focus on. The company will also host a webcast at 10.00 am ET today, that webcast can be found here.

The growth profile of the company is very impressive, and it is important to point out that the chart below does not include the Lynn Lake project, which is expected to come online in 2028-2029. Lynn Lake will boost the annual gold production towards 900Koz and lower costs further. On top of that, Alamos Gold is expected to release its growth plan for the Island Gold District after 2027 by the end of this year. The expected additional growth at the Island Gold district is likely to take the consolidated gold production to around 1Moz late this decade.

Few gold miners have had a more impressive operating performance than Alamos Gold, which is why the stock price has had such a strong performance over the last few years. With that said, the stock price has lagged the regular gold mining ETFs slightly this year following the less impressive Q1 result.

Q2 2025 Result

Q1 was an underwhelming quarter, with weaker production, and higher than expected costs. Q2 saw significant improvements. Alamos Gold reported record sales of $438M, record adjusted EBITDA of $260M, and record adjusted net earnings of $144M in the second quarter of the year. The financials saw substantial increases compared to the prior quarter and the company generated a healthy free cash flow as well. Alamos Gold beat the consensus EPS estimate of $0.33 in Q2 marginally as well.

The record realized gold price of $3,223/oz was the main factor in the record financials. The improved production and costs played a part as well. Alamos Gold reported a gold production of 137Koz in Q2, and the production level is expected to increase further during the second half of the year due to improved grades and higher milling rates.

The cash cost was $1,075/oz, and the all-in sustaining cost ("AISC") was $1,475/oz in Q2 2025, which were 10% and 18% lower than Q1. While costs showed significant improvements compared to Q1 and we can expect material improvements in the second half of the year, the higher gold price and slow start to the year has caused Alamos Gold to raise its costs guidance in 2025. The new AISC guidance in 2025 is $1,400-1,450/oz, but the production guidance remains unchanged.

The combination of a record realized gold price and lower costs led to record margins for Alamos Gold in Q2. The company reported an AISC margin of 1,748/oz, which is impressive. The EBITDA margin was a record 59% and the profit margin was as high as 33% in the most recent quarter.

Given that the gold price has continued to be strong in July, the company is guiding for higher production and lower costs in the second half of this year, and further improvements in the coming years. We are likely to see the stellar margins continue for Alamos Gold.

Production increased 10% quarter-over-quarter for Alamos Gold, and the production increases were relatively similar at the three mining districts. There was a 12% increase at the Mulatos District and 9% increases at the Island Gold District & Young-Davidson. All three mining districts are tracking slightly below their annual guidance ranges, but that was expected due to higher production from higher milling rates and better grades in the second half of the year and in Q4 especially.

The cash cost declined quarter-over-quarter for all three mining districts, a more substantial 18% decline at the Mulatos District, 9% at Young-Davidson, and 6% at the Island Gold District. All three mines are still tracking above the annual guidance ranges, which was expected due to higher milling rates and improved grades in the second half of the year.

Overall, Q2 was a solid quarter with significant improvements compared to Q1. It was roughly in line with expectations, and we can expect slight improvements in Q3 and more substantial improvements in Q4. The Island Gold underground ore is now from July being processed at the new Magino mill, which is an important milestone.

Conclusion

The price of gold has this year averaged just above $3,100/oz and if we use a gold price assumption of $3,000/oz for the next few years together with the higher end of cost guidance. The company is roughly projected to generate around $900M to $1.2B in operating cash flow over the next few years.

That translates to a sustaining free cash flow yield of 7-9% for Alamos Gold, which is in my view a very attractive valuation for a gold mining company with one of the best operating histories in the industry. Granted, most of the cash flow is today being invested to grow operations, which I have no objections to with this management team that has shown itself to be an excellent capital allocator.

I rate Alamos Gold a buy at this level, and I have been adding to my position around $25 earlier this month.

The main risk is the commodity price risk. Alamos Gold will be producing gold at a healthy margin using a gold price well below what we have seen lately, but it will not be immune to a lower gold price.

The stellar growth profile and excellent liquidity position, where the growth is expected to be funded by operating cash flow, likely means it has slightly better downside protection than the average gold mining company. Alamos Gold has a positive net cash position and only a minor amount of debt on the books, so the balance sheet is very strong as well.

There are also some risks of delays and costs overruns for the various growth projects. We have seen some development delays lately, where Lynn Lake has had construction halted due to wildfires recently and completion is now projected for the second half of 2028. The Phase 3+ Expansion at the Island Gold District is expected to be completed in the second half of next year.

The latest mine plan has Island Gold producing 411Koz annually from the end of 2026 with a mine-site AISC of $915/oz over the initial 12 years. Those numbers do not include the latest expansion study, which is due later this year and is likely to take the annual gold production at the Island Gold District above 500Koz. This would make it an even more unique asset.

data.title: Armstrong World Industries: Hold Rated Because Valuation Is Too High
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a hold rating for Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI). The business is executing very well, with strong pricing power in mineral fiber and impressive margin expansion in architectural specialties. Innovation efforts like ProjectWorks and TEMPLOK are building a durable moat. However, at ~25x forward earnings, the bar is high and leaves little room for error. The risk/reward is not attractive enough for a buy rating at this point.

Company Description

AWI is a US-based architectural product manufacturer used in commercial spaces. Its business is split into two key segments. The Mineral Fiber segment focuses on traditional ceiling tiles made from mineral fiber and fiberglass. It also includes AWI’s 50% stake in WAVE, a joint venture that produces the ceiling grid systems used to suspend those tiles. The other business is Architectural Specialties, which offers higher-end design solutions across ceilings, walls, and exteriors using materials like wood, metal, felt, and resin. These products are typically sold into the commercial construction market via distributors and direct channels.

2Q25 Earnings Review: Outperformance Signals Strong Momentum

AWI delivered a very solid performance in 2Q25. Total revenue saw 16.3% y/y growth to $424.6 million, marking AWI’s highest-ever second-quarter sales. Both segments contributed meaningfully: Mineral Fiber sales grew a solid 6.7% y/y to $267 million, while Architectural Specialties revenue surged 37.2% y/y to $157.6 million. Consequently, EBIT jumped 29.7% y/y to $123.2 million, with margin expanded by 300 bps to 29%. Adj. EBITDA grew 23.2% y/y to $154 million, with margin up 200 bps to a record 36.3%. Adj. EPS also grew 29% y/y to $2.09, marking the fastest quarterly earnings growth since 4Q16.

Mineral Fiber

The Mineral Fiber segment performance has proven itself to be a steady grower within AWI’s portfolio, and I don’t expect this narrative to turn anytime soon. The sales growth of 6.7% y/y in 2Q25 was driven largely by a 5% increase in Average Unit Value [AUV], which reflects the market’s willingness to pay for AWI’s premium, value-added offerings. Management has also given a very encouraging guide, now expecting AUV to grow >6% for the full year, implying an acceleration from 2Q25. This pricing strength has a very high margin impact as it flows through at 100% incremental cost to the EBITDA line. You can see from the numbers that adj. EBITDA for the segment grew 16% y/y, with margin expanding 350 bps to 45.2% (this is a level not seen since the pre-COVID peak in 4Q19).

The other key aspect is the consistency in execution by management. 2Q25 marks the tenth straight quarter of y/y margin expansion. This tells us the margin expansion strength was not driven by one-off tailwinds, but a structural one, driven by cost discipline and productivity gains. If you work the math backwards, volume for this segment was only up low single digits, and while that seems less bullish on a standalone basis, knowing that AWI still manages to drive up earnings with margin expansion is very encouraging.

Architectural Specialties

As for architectural specialties, this is the real highlight for 2Q25. The 37% y/y growth was solid, but what was even more notable is the 15% organic growth, which was faster than end-market growth rates and confirms that AWI is successfully executing on its strategy to win more wallet share “across more areas of the building” (by selling more complete solutions). This isn’t just about market recovery—it’s about real share gains. Even more impressive is that the 37% growth translated into 61% adj. EBITDA growth, with margins expanding 310 bps to ~22%, the highest second-quarter level since 4Q19. This margin ramp tells us a few things: (1) there are high incremental margins; and (2) the impact from high-margin custom projects is playing out. Management now guides for FY25 margins to land >20%, which, in my opinion, feels very plausible. Right now, this segment is still a relatively small piece of AWI’s business (18% of 1H25 EBIT), but at this pace of growth, this should become a core earnings growth driver. As this happens, it should also support a re-rating of AWI’s multiple as investors give more credit to the diversification.

Innovation and Digital Tools Create a Competitive Moat

AWI’s digital tools and product innovation help to shape up its competitive edge, I believe. Take ProjectWorks, for instance. This is AWI’s digital design platform, which I see as a major differentiator vs. peers. The idea is simple, in that it makes it easier for architects to design complex ceilings while simplifying installation for contractors. This effectively removes friction across the project cycle, which drives up both customer satisfaction and product pull-through (i.e., AWI drives demand for its product further down the value chain). The impact is already visible. In a recent four-story health center project in Virginia, ProjectWorks supported all seven phases, helping the client accelerate timelines and reduce complexity.

Financially, this drives a mix shift. By streamlining the design of complex ceiling systems, it pushes customers toward higher-AUV, higher-margin Architectural Specialties products. It also increases AWI’s influence at the specification stage, improving win rates and embedding its products earlier in the decision process. When competing against sub-scale players, this kind of digital advantage gives AWI a scalable edge, one that supports margin expansion and deepens customer stickiness.

Another example is AWI’s development of TEMPLOK. This is the industry’s first energy-saving ceiling tile, which I expect to enable AWI to tap into the growing demand for sustainable building materials. From the user perspective, the cost saving is clear—this tile material can reduce the energy required for heating and cooling a building by up to 15%. This directly addresses the growing demand from building owners seeking solutions to improve energy efficiency and achieve decarbonization. Furthermore, AWI has successfully integrated TEMPLOK into the energy modeling software platform from Integrated Environmental Solutions [IES]. This is an important step, as it introduces the ceiling plane as a new and viable source of energy savings to the architects and engineers who use the software to design buildings. I expect this to significantly accelerate awareness and adoption of the product. As you can expect, this is a higher-AUV product, which means it also has a similar positive mix shift impact on margins. As such, over time, it should lift margins within the mineral fiber segment.

Valuation

AWI’s valuation paints an entirely different story, though. While fundamentals are great, it is unfortunate that valuation is expensive. AWI forward PE is at ~25x today, and compared to its past decade valuation range, it is near an all-time high. The last time this happened, multiples plummeted sharply to as low as 13x in less than two years. I am not saying the same will happen, but the bar is high. Consensus is expecting adj. EPS to grow at a low-teens percentage for the next two years (FY26/27), and to play devil’s advocate, let’s assume consensus is right (they have been quite accurate over the past years) and that AWI prints $9 adj. EPS in FY27. Multiply that by 25x, and it equates to a price target of $225, or 19% upside. But should AWI miss expectations or show signs of growth weakness, multiple could revert very quickly to much lower. Assume it trades back to its average of 18x, and AWI misses estimates by, say, 10% (readers can adjust this to match their own conservatism); this implies a price target of $146, or 23% downside. This risk-reward is not compelling to me; as such, I am not giving a buy rating to AWI.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating on AWI. The growth story is credible and supported by strong execution across both segments, particularly in architectural specialties. But with valuation near historical highs and a fairly balanced risk/reward profile, I think it’s best to stay on the sidelines for now. A pullback or more clarity on sustained EPS acceleration could create a better entry point.

data.title: CRH plc: New Acquisition Part Of Strategic Pivot
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Propelled by the cyclical surge of construction material prices, CRH plc (NYSE:CRH) has been riding the trend and continues to grow even with the prices stagnant by using vertical and horizontal integration through acquisitions and divestitures. Its new strategic pivot towards higher margins and more sustainable solutions could help it to weather better in the future. But the integration risks and cash drain have started to creep in. The trade deal with the EU could also hamper its ability to fully utilize its transatlantic network. We want to see more details in the upcoming earnings release before jumping in and recommend a hold for now.

Analysis

CRH is the largest building materials company in North America and a global leader, holding #1 positions in asphalt and aggregates in North America, and strong regional leadership in Europe and Australia across various product lines such as cement, concrete, asphalt, etc. The company has revenue from the Americas Division, which comprises Americas Materials Solutions and Americas Building Solutions, and the International Division. Its Americas Division generates the largest revenue at 65% of the revenue, and the rest is from International. And between these divisions, since their business is so clearly defined by their regions, even outside sources rank the company by that division's performance for the region. It ranks #5 in America in the top 25 biggest construction material manufacturers by BuildOps, and #4 in the world in the same category.

Within the Americas Division, there are two segments with very different growth dynamics. The Americas Material Solutions segment has the high-growth, high-margin feature. In Q1, this segment reported a 293% increase in adj. EBITDA, and a 190 bps gain in margin. The America Building Solutions segment, on the other hand, reported a contraction of 7% in adj. EBITDA and the margin shrank by 110 bps in the same quarter. Both segments' revenue was more or less flat.

For its International Solutions segment, the revenue grew 7% QoQ with 22% higher in its adj. EBITDA and a 70 bps increase in its margin, mostly from positive underlying demand in central and eastern Europe, and some improved activity in Western Europe's infrastructure and non-residential demands.

CRH plc's revenue has experienced significant ups and downs in the past few years due to its acquisitions and divestitures. The company's revenue on a TTM basis went from around $28 billion in 2020 to above $35 billion currently.

Compared with the basic material index by FRED, it shows that CRH's business is greatly dominated by the cyclicality of the construction market. The about 50% rise of the construction material producer price index helped boost the company's revenue by almost 75%. However, with the index staying flat since 2022, while CRH's revenue has continued growing.

One of the factors to drive the growth is that CRH has been making aggressive acquisitions and divestitures in vertical and horizontal integration. For 2024 alone, the company had made $5 billion in acquisitions totally, with $3.8 billion spent in the Americas and $1.2 billion in International. It acquired "an attractive portfolio of cement and readymixed concrete operations" in Texas for $2.1 billion, according to its 10K of 2024. At the same time, it made $1.4 billion in divestiture primarily in the European lime business. The company's global scale provides significant operational efficiencies, purchasing power, and a diversified revenue base across multiple geographies and product categories. This makes it more resilient to regional downturns and also creates opportunities in vertical and horizontal integration on multiple fronts. This marks the first time its total assets have had a significant growth of about 15% since 2020.

CRH made 20 acquisitions in the Americas Material Solutions segment, 10 in Americas Building Solutions, and 9 in International Solutions. All the acquisitions and divestitures require constant integration efforts to boost efficiency and mitigate costs. So far, its track record has shown that it has been able to grow both the top line and the bottom line, with its EBITDA rising from around $2.8 billion to close to $7 billion.

Strong exposure to infrastructure spending in recent years provided robust growth momentum. CRH is a primary beneficiary of the public infrastructure spending boom, particularly in North America. The U.S. Bipartisan Infrastructure Law is expected to drive significant investment in roads, bridges, and critical infrastructure for years to come. CRH's strong presence in asphalt and aggregates, which are the key materials for roads, positions it advantageously. Moreover, its materials are also essential for complex non-residential construction and re-industrialization activities, such as manufacturing facilities, data centers, which are seeing positive underlying demand. These help to cushion the company from the slowdown in residential home construction since 2022.

A strategic shift towards higher-margin and sustainable solutions is another way to help CRH withstand the cyclicality. The company is strategically pivoting towards higher-margin infrastructure projects and value-added solutions, moving beyond purely cyclical materials production. This includes a focus on smart water management systems, low-carbon cement production, and precision-engineered construction solutions. It just announced yesterday that it is acquiring Eco Material Technologies for $2.1 billion, which operates a network of fresh and harvested fly ash and green cement operations through processing and recycling, along with synthetic gypsum and other materials. This is part of the company's commitment to ESG leadership, with targets like a 30% carbon reduction by 2030 and significant recycling efforts with 44.7 million tonnes of waste recycled in 2024. This aligns with global sustainability trends, attracting environmentally conscious investors and potentially securing more projects that prioritize green solutions.

Financial Overview & Valuation

CRH plc has a price-to-operating income ratio of 16.44x, which tugs it right in the middle compared with Holcim AG (OTCPK:HCMLY), Heidelberg Materials AG (OTCPK:HDLMY), Vulcan Materials Company (VMC), Martin Marietta Materials (MLM), and CEMEX, S.A.B. de C.V. (CX). Its stock price has been up by 4.32% YTD, during which it dropped by almost 10% YTD in April. The tariff will cause higher inflation, which in turn raises the cost of goods for CRH.

For the upcoming earnings release, we want to hear more details of this new deal that it has just closed. The acquisition just announced yesterday is a large one, comparable in size to the deal in Texas last year. We want to see how it plans on integrating Eco Materials into its core operations. Moreover, since the trade deal with EU has been struck, although it is mostly in the US' favor, the barriers will only make it harder, not easier, for the company to integrate its business across the Atlantic Ocean. Also, the negative net margin in Q1 is the first time in a while for CRH. And its cash-to-debt ratio by the end of last quarter is only at 22%. It is likely to be lower in the new release. We want to see how all the trade policies play out for the company and how the acquisitions made in the past 18 months integrate well into its core operations. For these reasons, we recommend a Hold for now.

Conclusion

CRH plc's dominant size and network give it the scale and resources it needs to achieve better growth and efficiency, but also make it susceptible to the construction material market's cyclicality. Its aggressive vertical and horizontal integration aims at achieving better growth and pivoting towards higher-margin and sustainable solutions, but also leaves it with low cash and integration risks. We would like to see more details regarding the latest deal it made and how management views the trans-Atlantic trade deal would have an impact on its business, and would recommend waiting on the sidelines for now.
